paintings.title: Allegory of Divine Providence and Barberini Power
paintings.story: This vast fresco fills the ceiling of the grand salon of the Palazzo Barberini, the palatial home of the family of Maffeo Barberini, by then Pope Urban VIII. Begun in 1633, it was nearly finished within three years, then extensively reworked to completion in 1639 after Cortona's return from Venice. At one end of the open sky sits the radiant figure of Divine Providence, while putti and flying maidens hold aloft the papal keys and tiara — the whole scene teeming with the suns and bees of the Barberini arms.
funFacts: The Barberini family coat of arms carried three bees, which swarm through the fresco's imagery.
Urban VIII had launched an extensive programme to refurbish Rome with art and architecture, and this ceiling became one of its defining monuments.

paintings.title: The Rape of the Sabine Women
paintings.story: In 1626 the Sacchetti family engaged Cortona to paint three large canvases — The Sacrifice of Polyxena, The Triumph of Bacchus, and this Rape of the Sabines of about 1629. The subject, from Livy's account of early Rome, shows the moment Romulus's men seize the Sabine women to secure wives for the new city. It was in the orbit of the Sacchetti that Cortona met Pope Urban VIII and Cardinal Francesco Barberini, whose patronage would transform his career.
funFacts: Cortona depicted the abduction of the Sabine women at least twice.
The Capitoline picture gallery that houses it also holds paintings by Caravaggio, Titian, Rubens and Annibale Carracci.

paintings.title: Romulus and Remus Taken in by Faustulus
paintings.story: Painted around 1643, this canvas shows the shepherd Faustulus bringing the twin founders of Rome into his household. It is one of three works Cortona contributed — alongside six by other artists — to the gilded gallery of the hôtel de La Vrillière in Paris, commissioned by Louis Phélypeaux, Seigneur of La Vrillière. Sold with the mansion in 1705 and passed through the Bourbon line, it was seized by the French revolutionary state in 1794 and entered the Louvre.
funFacts: The painting is also known as Faustulus Entrusting Romulus and Remus to Larentia, and as Remus Discovered.

paintings.title: The Golden Age
paintings.location: Sala della Stufa, Palazzo Pitti, Florence
paintings.story: Passing through Florence in 1637, Cortona was asked by Ferdinando II de' Medici, Grand Duke of Tuscany, to paint a series of frescoes representing Ovid's Four Ages of Man in the small Sala della Stufa of the Palazzo Pitti. The Golden Age and the Silver Age were painted first; Cortona was recalled in 1641 to add the Bronze and Iron Ages. He is said to have been guided in the allegorical designs by Michelangelo Buonarroti the Younger.
funFacts: The commission led on to Cortona's grand decoration of the Pitti's five Planetary Rooms for the Medici.
The advisor on the programme, Michelangelo Buonarroti the Younger, was the great sculptor's grandnephew.

paintings.title: The Silver Age
paintings.location: Sala della Stufa, Palazzo Pitti, Florence
paintings.story: The Silver Age was one of the first two frescoes — with the Golden Age — that Cortona painted in 1637 in the Sala della Stufa of the Palazzo Pitti for Ferdinando II de' Medici, in a cycle representing Ovid's Four Ages of Man. The 'ages' of bronze and iron followed when the painter was recalled to Florence in 1641.
funFacts: The cycle marks the start of Cortona's long involvement with the decoration of the Palazzo Pitti, whose grand-ducal reception rooms he later frescoed.

paintings.title: Ananias Heals Paul's Blindness
paintings.location: Santa Maria della Concezione dei Cappuccini, Rome
paintings.story: This altarpiece of about 1631 hangs in the first chapel on the left of Santa Maria della Concezione dei Cappuccini, the Capuchin church in Rome whose first stone was blessed by Pope Urban VIII in 1626 and whose construction was begun by the pope's Capuchin brother, Cardinal Antonio Marcello Barberini. It depicts the disciple Ananias restoring the sight of Saul of Tarsus — the future Apostle Paul — blinded on the road to Damascus.
funFacts: The same church is famous for its crypt, decorated with the bones of thousands of Capuchin friars.
Caravaggio's Conversion on the Way to Damascus, in Santa Maria del Popolo, depicts the moment of blinding that precedes this scene.

paintings.title: The Triumph of Bacchus
paintings.story: One of the three large canvases the Sacchetti family engaged Cortona to paint in 1626, together with The Sacrifice of Polyxena and The Rape of the Sabines. The wine god rides in triumph amid his noisy retinue. Marcello Sacchetti, papal treasurer under Urban VIII, had noticed the young painter after a copy he made of Raphael's Galatea fresco, and the family's patronage launched Cortona into the circle of the Barberini.
funFacts: Cortona painted frescoes for the Sacchetti at their villa in Castelfusano with a team that included the young Andrea Sacchi.

paintings.title: Ceiling frescoes of the Chiesa Nuova
paintings.story: For decades Cortona was involved in the decoration of the ceiling frescoes of the Oratorian Chiesa Nuova — Santa Maria in Vallicella — in Rome, a labour not finished until 1665. The church is the principal home of the Oratorians, the congregation founded by St Philip Neri in the era of the Counter-Reformation, and Cortona's ceiling frescoes crown its interior.
funFacts: The Chiesa Nuova frescoes were among the defining commissions of Cortona's late career, alongside frescoes in the Palazzo Pamphilj on Piazza Navona.
The 'New Church' was rebuilt for Philip Neri's congregation beginning in 1575.
